elements.sent-2411-001: For the population number at time $n=0,1,2,\ldots$, written $x_{n}$, we show that some exactly soluble cases of $x_{n}$ can be related to a different problem, where the number of individuals changes in continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-002: With $x(t)$ the population number at continuous time $t\geq0$, the relation is that $x(t=n)$ precisely coincides with $x_{n}$.
elements.sent-2411-003: In such a case, we call the two models equivalent, even though $x_{n}$ obeys a difference equation and $x(t)$ obeys a differential equation.
elements.sent-2411-004: Cases of equivalence can be found for both time homogeneous and time inhomogeneous equations.
elements.sent-2411-005: A comparison of the difference equation and the differential equation of the equivalent problem, allows us to make a correspondence between coefficients in these equations, thereby exposing structural similarities and differences of the two problems.
elements.sent-2411-006: When the equation that $x_{n}$ obeys is modified, e.g., by the introduction of time inhomogeneity, so $x_{n}$ has no exact solution, we show how to construct an approximate equivalent continuous time solution, $x^{(\mathrm{app ) (t)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: We find that $x^{(\mathrm{app ) (t=n)$ can yield a good approximation to $x_{n}$ because $x^{(\mathrm{app ) (t)$ encapsulates much of the behaviour of $x_{n}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: We note that some discrete time problems may have solutions with an oscillatory component that changes sign as $n\rightarrow n+1$.
elements.sent-2411-009: This occurs with the Fibonacci numbers.
elements.sent-2411-010: We find that the way an equivalent continuous time solution replicates this oscillatory behaviour is by becoming complex; it no longer represents a simple interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-011: There are many instances in biology and related subjects where a population exhibits change in discrete time.
elements.sent-2411-012: Two examples that come readily to mind are: (i) an evolving biological population with non-overlapping generations, such as an annual plant \cite{Maynardsmith , and (ii) the spread of a virus, within a population, when testing is carried out at regular times (such as every week), and knowledge of the number of infected individuals is known only at the times of testing \cite{Yakubu .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: These two examples have different interpretations of the discrete time-steps, but in what follows, we shall often describe discrete time-steps as generations, irrespective of the context.
elements.sent-2411-014: Consider a population that changes in discrete time.
elements.sent-2411-015: If a mathematical model of this population can be approximated by a model that exhibits change in continuous time, then typically, much more analytical information will be available about the behaviour of the population.
elements.sent-2411-016: A typical situation where discrete time can be well approximated by continuous time is when the dynamical quantity of interest, e.g., an gene frequency or population size/number, exhibits only small fractional (or percentage) changes from one generation to the next.
elements.sent-2411-017: Then, such an approximation can plausibly be made, and the difference equation, which describes the discrete time dynamics, becomes replaced by the corresponding continuous time description, namely a differential equation.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, what happens when changes in the dynamical quantity of interest do not have small fractional changes, from one generation to the next? In such cases, it would appear to be a very bad approximation to replace discrete time by continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-019: In the present work we introduce methodology that, in some cases, allows us to obtain a continuous time description of a discrete time problem, that is exact in the sense that when the continuous time equals a generation time (i.e., an integer) the discrete and continuous time problems have solutions that precisely agree.
elements.sent-2411-020: We shall describe the discrete and continuous time problems, when this precise agreement occurs as being equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-021: When such a continuous time description exists that is equivalent to a discrete time problem, we learn the generally nontrivial relationship between coefficients of terms in the discrete time problem and related coefficients in the equivalent continuous time problem.
elements.sent-2411-022: This equivalence of discrete and continuous time problems is not solely when there is time homogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-023: As we shall show in some examples, there are cases of time-dependent parameters that also lead to equivalence (i.e., exact agreement of the discrete and continuous time problems at discrete generation times).
elements.sent-2411-024: The equivalent continuous time analogue of an exactly soluble discrete time problem can capture quite extreme behaviours of the discrete time solution, such as extremely rapid growth.
elements.sent-2411-025: We can exploit this, by considering a modification of an exactly soluble discrete time problem, so that it has no exact solution.
elements.sent-2411-026: The same (or very similar) modification of the equivalent continuous time problem, will now no longer be equivalent to the modified discrete time problem.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, this continuous time problem may serve as a useful starting point for elucidating the behaviour of the modified discrete time problem, since it may encapsulate a very large amount of the behaviour of the discrete time problem.
elements.sent-2411-028: We shall explore some examples of this approach in this work.
elements.sent-2411-029: Beyond this, while the equivalent continuous time solution may be viewed as simply an interpolation of the discrete time solution, that precisely agrees at discrete times, this is not the full story.
elements.sent-2411-030: For some discrete time problems, there may be an oscillatory component that changes sign often.
elements.sent-2411-031: We give examples where an exactly soluble discrete time solution gives rise to an equivalent continuous time solution.

elements.sent-2411-109: In this section we illustrate the above procedure, of going from discrete to continuous time, in a model from genetics.
elements.sent-2411-110: We consider a very large (effectively infinite) population of asexual organisms, that carry one gene, which can be either $A$ or $B$ (a stricter term for $A$ and $B$ is alleles, but we shall use the term gene in this work).
elements.sent-2411-111: The population has discrete generations, labelled by $n=0,1,2,\ldots$.

elements.sent-2411-277: Two examples are: (i) a biological population with non-overlapping generations, such as an annual plant, which lives just for one year, during which it reproduces \cite{Maynardsmith , and (ii) the spread of a viral disease within the human population, when testing is carried out at regular times (such as every week), and knowledge of the number of infected individuals is known only at the times of testing \cite{Yakubu .}}
elements.sent-2411-278: Different examples may have very different interpretations of the discrete time-steps.
elements.sent-2411-279: However, in what follows, we shall describe discrete time-steps as generations, irrespective of the context.
elements.sent-2411-280: Let us consider first the situation where discrete time can be well approximated by continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-281: This typically occurs when the dynamical quantity of interest, e.g., a population size, exhibits only small fractional (or percentage) changes from one generation to the next.
elements.sent-2411-282: Then such an approximation can plausibly be made, and the difference equation, which describes the discrete time dynamics, becomes replaced by the corresponding continuous time description, namely a differential equation.
elements.sent-2411-283: However, when the dynamical quantity of interest does not exhibit small fractional changes, from one generation to the next, it would appear to be a very bad approximation to replace discrete time by continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-284: In the present work we introduce methodology that allows us, in some cases, to obtain a continuous time description of a discrete time problem that is exact.
elements.sent-2411-285: By `exact' we mean that if the continuous time is set equal to a specific generation (i.e., an integer), then the continuous time solution precisely agrees with the solution of the discrete time problem at the specific generation.
elements.sent-2411-286: When this precise agreement occurs, we describe the discrete and continuous time problems as being equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-287: When we find the continuous time system that is `equivalent' to a discrete time system, one of the key results that we obtain is a generally non-trivial relationship between coefficients in the discrete time dynamical equation and the corresponding coefficients in the continuous time equation.
elements.sent-2411-288: As we shall show in some examples, this equivalence of discrete and continuous time problems is not solely when there is time homogeneity, and the coefficients are independent of time.
elements.sent-2411-289: There are cases of time-dependent coefficients that also lead to a continuous time problem being equivalent to a discrete time problem.
elements.sent-2411-290: We note that an equivalent continuous time analogue of an exactly soluble discrete time problem can precisely capture very extreme behaviours of the discrete time problem.
elements.sent-2411-291: For example, the continuous time solution can capture extremely rapid growth in the dynamical quantity of interest of a discrete time problem, where there are large fractional changes from one generation to the next.
elements.sent-2411-292: We can exploit this, by considering a modification of an exactly soluble discrete time problem, to one with no exact solution, and find an approximate equivalent problem.
elements.sent-2411-293: This continuous time problem may serve as a useful approach to elucidating the behaviour of the modified discrete time problem; a very large amount of the behaviour of the discrete time is encapsulated by the continuous time problem, and analytical methods for continuous time can be employed in its analysis.
elements.sent-2411-294: This leads to approximate results for the modified discrete time problem.
elements.sent-2411-295: For some discrete time problems, there may be an oscillatory component that rapidly changes with time.
elements.sent-2411-296: We give examples where exactly soluble discrete time problems with oscillations give rise to equivalent continuous time problems.
elements.sent-2411-297: However, to define the continuous time solutions, we find they need to be complex to precisely capture the oscillations.
elements.sent-2411-298: This work thus non-trivially relates some discrete and continuous time problems, shows how to exploit this relationship in `close' cases, where analytical methods of continuous time may be employed, and shows how to deal with the issue of rapidly changing solutions in discrete time.

elements.sent-2411-423: Examples include the size of a population when generations are non-overlapping, and the incidence of a disease when infections are recorded at fixed intervals.
elements.sent-2411-424: For discrete-time systems lacking exact solutions, continuous-time approximations are frequently employed when small changes occur between discrete time steps.
elements.sent-2411-425: Here, we present an approach motivated by exactly soluble discrete time problems.
elements.sent-2411-426: We show that such systems have continuous-time descriptions (governed by differential equations) whose solutions precisely agree, at the discrete times, with the discrete time solutions, irrespective of the size of changes that occur.
elements.sent-2411-427: For discrete-time systems lacking exact solutions, we develop approximate continuous-time models that can, to high accuracy, capture rapid growth and decay.
elements.sent-2411-428: Our approach employs mappings between difference and differential equations, generating functional solutions that exactly or closely preserve the original discrete time behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-429: It uncovers fundamental structural parallels and also distinctions between the difference equation and the `equivalent' differential equation.
elements.sent-2411-430: The findings we present cover both time-homogeneous and time-inhomogeneous systems.
elements.sent-2411-431: For completeness, we also consider discrete-time systems with the most rapid oscillatory behaviour possible, namely a sign change each time step.
elements.sent-2411-432: We show, for exactly soluble cases, that such systems also have a continuous-time description, but that this comes at the expense of generally complex-valued solutions.
elements.sent-2411-433: This work has applications in, for example, population genetics, ecology and epidemic modelling.
elements.sent-2411-434: By bridging discrete and continuous representations of a system, it enhances insights/analysis of different types of dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-435: Discrete time dynamics arise naturally in biological systems.
elements.sent-2411-436: Classical examples include: annual plant populations with non-overlapping generations \cite{Maynardsmith ; disease surveillance data collected at fixed intervals \cite{Yakubu, earn2000simple ; seasonal diseases such as measles \cite{earn2000simple , which can exhibit near periodic variation; pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic modelling for discrete dosing \cite{mager2003diversity .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-437: Each of these applications illustrates biological phenomena that involve discrete or near discrete events, possibly within the context of continuous time systems.
elements.sent-2411-438: Discrete time dynamics is usually described by a difference equation.
elements.sent-2411-439: A continuous time approximation follows by first approximating the difference equation by a differential equation, and then finding the continuous time solution.
elements.sent-2411-440: Such an approximation is commonly used when changes between discrete time steps are small.
elements.sent-2411-441: However, some biological systems can exhibit appreciable changes between time steps, e.g., viral load doubling, rapid spread of a large $R$ infection, and geometric growth with an appreciable growth factor.
elements.sent-2411-442: Generally, continuous time approximations fail for systems with large per-step changes, limiting their utility in real-world applications like epidemiology or genetics.
elements.sent-2411-443: In this work we first establish an exact correspondence between the solutions and equations of some exactly soluble discrete time systems and the mathematically distinct equations/solutions of some continuous time systems.
elements.sent-2411-444: An outcome is that a difference equation describing a discrete time system becomes replaced by a differential equation, whose solution precisely agrees, at the discrete times, with the discrete time solution.
elements.sent-2411-445: We then go beyond exactly soluble discrete-time problems.
elements.sent-2411-446: We establish an approximate correspondence between discrete and continuous time systems.
elements.sent-2411-447: This amounts to an approximation scheme for discrete-time problems that are not exactly soluble.
elements.sent-2411-448: The approximation is expressed in terms of the solution of a differential equation, but now the solutions only approximately agree.
elements.sent-2411-449: The above approach, which derives either an exact or an approximate differential equation to describe a discrete time system, is, as we shall show, often able to handle extreme parameter values that lead to large inter-step changes.
elements.sent-2411-450: This bypasses the need for restrictive small-parameter assumptions for validity of the differential equation.
elements.sent-2411-451: A further feature of the above approach is that it works for both time homogeneous and time inhomogeneous problems.
elements.sent-2411-452: This then becomes relevant to some real world discrete time cases with time-dependent parameters (e.g., seasonal disease transmission).
elements.sent-2411-453: For completeness, we also consider a class of discrete-time systems that display the most rapid oscillatory behaviour possible in such systems, namely where the dynamical quantity changes sign at every time step.
elements.sent-2411-454: While it would seem impossible to describe such systems in continuous time, we explicitly show, in exactly soluble problems.
elements.sent-2411-455: However the solutions are generally complex-valued, with the exception of the discrete times, when the solutions are real and there is complete agreement with the discrete time solution.
elements.sent-2411-456: This work non-trivially relates some discrete and continuous time problems, and shows how to exploit this relationship in `close' cases.
elements.sent-2411-457: It further shows how to deal with the issue of rapidly changing solutions in discrete time.
elements.sent-2411-458: The relationship between discrete and continuous time models has implications in various biological contexts.
elements.sent-2411-459: In population dynamics there are discrete and continuous time descriptions \cite{caswell2001matrix, kot2001elements ; in pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic analyses, drug dosing can be carried out discretely (via bolus dosing) or continuously; in epidemiology, both discrete and continuous time models are used \cite{earn2000simple, scutt2022theoretically, Guerra .}}
